name: Paula Scher
description: Paula Scher ran CBS Records in the 70s (200+ album covers), joined Pentagram in 1991, and built the Public Theater's enduring identity. Her work is typographic maximalism with structure — big type, broken grids, collage sensibility without chaos. The anti-Vignelli within the modernist tradition: same rigor, more play. Her work hangs in MoMA and covers NYC.
rules: Type is architecture. A single word can occupy 60% of a poster.
Overlap is expressive — type layered on type, words touching, negative and positive space fighting.
Break the grid for a reason. Scher's work isn't random — every off-grid choice has a visual argument.
Condensed display typefaces are the native voice (Knockout, Titling Gothic, Bureau Grotesque).
Two-color printing discipline — Scher's CBS era worked with extreme budget constraints; the limitation shaped the voice.
Rotation is a tool — headlines set at 8°, 15°, 22° rotation, used like a spatial emphasis.
Motion: headline-driven entry, letters stagger-reveal, never bouncy or soft.
Typography scale is massive — display at 10vw minimum, body shrinks to stay out of the way.
